name: Algal Spot
description: Algal Spot, also known as Red Rust, is common in tea fields with high humidity, mist, shade, and poor air movement. It causes orange or green patches on tea leaves and reduces photosynthesis, shoot growth, and leaf quality.
highRiskTreatments: Remove and destroy heavily infected leaves and branches
Prune infected branches to improve air circulation
Reduce excessive shade and allow better sunlight penetration
Improve field drainage to reduce moisture around bushes
Spray Bordeaux mixture as recommended by tea field officers
Spray copper oxychloride if infection is spreading rapidly
Avoid plucking wet leaves during severe infection periods
Maintain clean field sanitation by removing fallen infected leaves
Apply compost or well-decomposed organic matter to improve plant strength
Monitor fields closely after rainy or misty days
lowRiskTreatments: Improve sunlight exposure by light pruning
Clear weeds around tea bushes to improve airflow
Apply organic compost to improve plant health
Use neem-based foliar support where suitable
Record affected areas for follow-up inspection

name: Brown Blight
description: Brown Blight is a fungal tea disease that causes brown lesions, drying, and poor leaf quality. It spreads faster in warm, humid, and wet field conditions.
highRiskTreatments: Remove infected leaves and dispose of them away from the field
Maintain clean plucking practices
Disinfect pruning knives and plucking tools
Prune dense tea bushes to improve ventilation
Improve drainage in waterlogged areas
Spray Trifloxystrobin if recommended by an authorized officer
Spray Azoxystrobin where fungal infection is severe
Use copper-based fungicide only according to approved recommendations
Avoid excessive nitrogen fertilizer during infection
Apply potassium-rich balanced fertilizer to strengthen plants
Remove dead leaves and plant debris from the field
Monitor new shoots after treatment
lowRiskTreatments: Remove affected leaves at early stage
Improve air circulation through light pruning
Avoid plucking during wet conditions when possible
Use neem-based organic sprays for general field hygiene
Avoid spreading infected leaves during field work

name: Gray Blight
description: Gray Blight causes grayish brown lesions on tea leaves, leaf drying, and leaf fall. It is common under high humidity, frequent rainfall, and poor drainage.
highRiskTreatments: Remove infected leaves and fallen plant debris
Prune dense canopy to reduce humidity
Improve drainage immediately in wet areas
Spray copper fungicides as recommended
Use approved systemic fungicide if disease spreads rapidly
Avoid excessive shade in affected blocks
Disinfect tools after pruning infected plants
Apply balanced fertilizer to improve recovery
Use compost to improve soil condition
Avoid moving infected plant material to healthy areas
Monitor after rainfall and misty periods
lowRiskTreatments: Increase spacing and airflow where possible
Remove fallen leaves from the base of bushes
Reduce dense shade trees if needed

name: Helopeltis
description: Helopeltis is an insect pest that attacks tender tea shoots, buds, and young leaves. It causes feeding marks, curling, shoot damage, and yield reduction.
highRiskTreatments: Remove badly damaged tender shoots
Monitor pest population in young shoots and shaded areas
Encourage natural predators and beneficial insects
Spray neem-based botanical insecticide at early infestation
Use sticky traps or field monitoring traps where suitable
Spray Dimethoate only if recommended and legally approved
Spray Profenofos only under officer guidance for severe infestation
Avoid unnecessary broad-spectrum insecticide use
Remove unmanaged nearby host vegetation
Maintain regular plucking to reduce pest shelter
Inform tea field officer if pest spread is severe
lowRiskTreatments: Spray neem oil or neem extract
Encourage spiders and other natural predators
Keep tea plants clean and well-pruned
Remove weed hosts around the field
Use light monitoring before chemical control

name: Red Spot
description: Red Spot causes reddish or brown leaf spots in tea. It is usually encouraged by wet leaves, high humidity, shade, and poor air circulation.
highRiskTreatments: Remove infected leaves and dispose safely
Spray Bordeaux mixture if infection spreads
Spray copper fungicide according to approved recommendations
Reduce shade and improve sunlight
Prune dense bushes to improve ventilation
Improve drainage around affected bushes
Avoid plucking during wet leaf conditions
Disinfect field tools after use
Apply compost to improve plant vigor
Maintain proper spacing and field sanitation

name: Healthy
description: The tea plant appears healthy with no visible disease symptoms.
lowRiskTreatments: Maintain proper pruning and plucking cycles
Apply balanced fertilizer according to recommendation
Apply compost or organic matter to improve soil health
Monitor after heavy rain or misty weather
Inspect young shoots for pest activity

name: Bacterial Leaf Blight
description: Bacterial Leaf Blight causes yellowing and drying of rice leaves, usually starting from leaf tips and margins. It is favored by rain, high humidity, flooding, and excess nitrogen.
highRiskTreatments: Remove severely infected plants where practical
Avoid moving water from infected fields to healthy fields
Maintain proper field drainage
Avoid excess nitrogen fertilizer
Apply balanced fertilizer with potassium support
Use resistant rice varieties recommended for the area
Use certified seed in the next season
Maintain clean irrigation water
Avoid clipping seedlings before transplanting
Remove infected crop residue after harvest
Do not apply unnecessary bactericides without officer advice
Inform agriculture officer if disease spreads rapidly
lowRiskTreatments: Remove infected leaves or plants where possible
Use compost to improve soil health
Use resistant varieties in future planting

name: Brown Spot
description: Brown Spot is a fungal rice disease common in weak or nutrient-stressed fields. It causes brown lesions and can reduce yield, especially under poor soil fertility.
highRiskTreatments: Remove infected leaves where possible
Apply compost or organic manure to improve soil fertility
Apply potassium fertilizer if deficiency is suspected
Apply zinc fertilizer if deficiency symptoms are present
Use certified seeds in the next season
Treat seeds with recommended fungicide before sowing
Spray Mancozeb when disease spreads under humid conditions
Spray Carbendazim only according to approved recommendation
Improve field drainage
Avoid plant stress from poor nutrition
Avoid excessive nitrogen without balanced nutrients
Monitor disease after rainfall
lowRiskTreatments: Check potassium and zinc deficiency symptoms

name: Leaf Blast
description: Leaf Blast is a serious fungal disease of rice. It spreads rapidly under high humidity, wet leaves, dense planting, and excess nitrogen.

name: Rice Tungro
description: Rice Tungro is a viral disease common in rice-growing areas and is spread mainly by green leafhoppers. It causes yellow-orange discoloration, stunted growth, reduced tillering, and yield loss.

Control green leafhoppers using recommended insecticides
